data.title: January Jobs Report: A Strong Rebound After A Soft 2025 Labor Market
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist and Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

The January employment report delivered a stronger-than-expected gain in payrolls, challenging concerns that the economy is losing momentum. Non-farm payrolls rose 130,000 in January, beating expectations and marking a sharp acceleration from December. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.3%, revealing labor market stabilization despite significant downward revisions to 2025 payrolls.

Structural forces, including aging demographics, constrained immigration, and AI-driven productivity gains, continue to suppress job creation. Even so, the jobs report reflects a labor market that remains resilient, with a broadening of industry job gains reinforcing the economy’s strength at the start of the year.

Report details

  • Total non-farm payrolls increased by 130,000 in January, surpassing expectations of 65,000. Revisions to November and December lowered total payroll growth by 17,000 jobs, a notable improvement from the previous two-month revision that subtracted 76,000 jobs. Private payrolls were robust, rising 172,000 versus expectations of 68,000. Together, January’s job gains suggest the labor market is beginning 2026 on firmer footing.
  • Payroll gains were led once again by healthcare & social assistance, which added 137,000 jobs, the largest monthly increase since mid-2020. These strong gains highlight the sector’s ongoing structural momentum and continued meaningful contribution to overall job creation. Gains in construction, manufacturing, and professional and business services - three sectors that lagged in 2025 - underscore the improving breadth of hiring and point to some stabilization in cyclical industries.
  • Payroll weakness was concentrated in government (-42,000), and financial activities (-22,000). Federal government jobs accounted for the bulk of government losses, largely driven by deferred resignations rather than cyclical deterioration. Losses in financial activities were centered in insurance carriers and related activities, as well as real estate and rental & leasing services.
  • The unemployment rate declined to 4.3%, down from 4.4% last month. Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate increased 0.1% from the prior month, signaling underlying resilience in labor demand despite ongoing challenges for job seekers re-entering the workforce. Combined with firm average hourly earnings, which grew by 3.7% over the past year, the data points to labor market stabilization rather than deterioration.
  • On a softer note, benchmark revisions, which are released every January, lowered 2025’s total job gains, indicating the labor market was weaker than initially reported. Although much of this had already been anticipated based on provisional estimates released a few months ago, today’s revisions were slightly more negative than expected. However, this softness should be viewed against structural forces - increasing retirements and tighter immigration policies - that lowered the pace of job growth needed to keep the unemployment rate steady (i.e., breakeven rate). In this context, payrolls at levels that would appear recessionary in prior years are less concerning amid a shrinking labor supply.

Policy outlook

A stronger-than-expected January payroll gain, a decline in the unemployment rate, and wage growth holding near 4% all highlight the labor market’s underlying resilience, further reducing the need for immediate monetary support. In fact, market expectations for the first 2026 rate cut have now shifted from June to July.

In the absence of a clear and sustained deceleration in inflation ahead, incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will likely face challenges in persuading the FOMC to adopt a more dovish stance.

The labor market, as it stands, will not make that case for him. We do, however, still expect the Fed to cut rates in the second half of the year to move policy back toward a neutral stance, neither accommodative nor restrictive.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: XPAY: Middle Of The Road ETF For High Yield Income From The S&P 500
data.description:
Overview

Roundhill S&P 500 Target 20 Managed ETF (XPAY) is a strategic high yield fund that aims to provide investors with the ability to participate in the upside of the market, while collecting a superior dividend yield. After covering a plethora of different high yield ETFs, I believe that there are some clear traits between funds that are efficient long-term positions, and those that are not. I believe that XPAY's approach can be a suitable long-term position for investors that want to implement a simple buy-and-hold approach. Compared to some similar peers, XPAY demonstrates better performance and capital preservation. However, the fund still gets outpaced by the highest quality choices in the high yield sector, which means that XPAY is a middle of the road choice.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that XPAY's share price has declined by about 8.8%. Despite the S&P 500 rising to all-time highs in the last quarter, XPAY's share price was incapable of providing a meaningful level of growth. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 14.6% over the same time frame. As stated in the fund's name, XPAY aims to issue an annualized distribution rate of 20%, while maintaining an emphasis on tax-efficiency. While this makes the fund an enticing choice for income investors, there are some clear tradeoffs that must be considered.

For instance, there should be no expectation for XPAY's performance to stay on par with the performance of a standard S&P500 ETF. Additionally, the fund's operating history is limited, so XPAY hasn't been time tested through different market cycles yet. I suspect that a prolonged downturn will result in XPAY eroding investor capital over time. While there is a chance that the distributions can help offset total losses over time, it really depends on how drastic of a market decline. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that XPAY implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, XPAY has total assets under management of ~$111M and an expense ratio of 0.49%. This expense ratio is higher than standard growth ETFs, but may be acceptable considering the active strategy and higher yield offered to investors. However, the underlying strategy is a bit complex since XPAY doesn't actually hold any equities or ETFs that make up the S&P 500 Index. So XPAY is essentially a synthetic fund that replicates the movement of the index through flexible exchange options.

Looking at the holdings below, we can confirm that XPAY doesn't actually provide any exposure to equities. As we can see, the fund chooses to write deep ITM (in-the-money) call options on the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), which helps the fund mirror the daily price changes of the index. The fund spreads its expiration dates and selected strike prices across a wide range to help cover all of its bases. The top three positions make up over ~48% of total assets:

  • SPY - 02/12/2027 - $33.47C
  • SPY - 12/11/2026 - $33.10C
  • SPY - 03/12/2027 - $33.67C

What differentiates XPAY from traditional covered call peers out there is the actual process for generating the amplified income levels needed to support the distributions. This is because XPAY generates cash by periodically selling or closing out these FLEX option positions when they are at a profit. This is different from other option ETFs that capture option premiums by sacrificing the upside growth of its holdings and harnessing volatility of its holdings. Instead, XPAY is just closing out profitable positions and rolling that capital back into new positions.

The main difference as a result of this strategy is that XPAY may technically be able to capture a greater level of upside through market rallies. Since XPAY is strategically built to move alongside the S&P 500 Index, it may be suitable choice for income investors that want to hold an income fund that still has the ability to participate in rallies.

Performance Insights & Risks

Although the fund's history is limited, we can already see some of its downsides when assessing the performance so far. Since inception, XPAY's share price has declined by 7.6%, despite market indices rising to all-time highs over the last quarter. The fund was still unable to recover from the initial market decline that took place in April of 2025. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, XPAY's total return since inception jumps up to 22%. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Despite the strategy's advanced approach to generating income, XPAY still remains vulnerable to capped upside growth like many other high yield funds. This is just the reality for these high yield funds, which is why these income funds may not be the best fit for all investors. I believe that XPAY is a great accompanying fund within an already diverse portfolio of assets. By choosing to make XPAY the only way you get exposure to the S&P 500, you are aligning yourself to underperformance over a longer holding period.

On a more positive note, XPAY has demonstrated the efficiency of its strategy when compared to peer funds. Although their overall strategy for generating income, portfolio strategy, and option writing approaches are different, the underlying premise of generating income from exposure to the S&P 500 remains the same. Two popular choices within this high yield space are the following:

  • Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE): ~16% dividend yield.
  • YieldMax S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (SDTY): ~31% dividend yield.

When looking at a performance comparison, we can see that XPAY's strategy is a lot more efficient at preserving capital compared to 0DTE (zero-day-to-expiration) alternatives. As we can see, XPAY has suffered the lowest price decline out of the group, while XDTE has suffered from the largest decline. Similarly, XPAY also has the highest total return of the group, despite having a lower dividend yield than SDTY at this time. So even though the fund's structure still has its flaws, the performance is an indication that XPAY could be a more suitable long-term holding.

One of the unfortunate realities of these high yield funds is that they can frequently pay out more than they earn. This is more likely to occur during periods where market declines and the fund has trouble generating income. So even though XPAY's total return has managed to stay closely aligned with SPY since its inception, we can see that the share price continues to erode. The issue is that XPAY will continue to issue high distributions, even if it didn't capture gains to support those payouts. So during periods of market declines, XPAY will experience NAV erosion.

Since XPAY doesn't actually have exposure to direct equities within the index, I believe that NAV erosion will continue to be a threat for the fund. Even through the strongest market rallies, XPAY was unable to recover from the prior market decline. So during a year of mild growth for the index, investors should limit their expectation for capital appreciation to occur. If you want exposure to a high yield fund that tracks the S&P 500, provides direct equity exposure to holdings within the index, and has the ability to provide capital appreciation, you are better off with the following alternatives:

  1. Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX): ~8% dividend yield.
  2. NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI): ~12% dividend yield.

When running a performance comparison, we can see how these alternatives offer a much healthier level of price growth over time. This is attributed to the fact that GPIX and SPYI both hold the equities within the S&P 500. So when the index rises, these funds are directly participating in that growth through its holdings, rather than trying to simulate that growth through flexible options.

Tax-Efficient Dividends

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.8995 per share, the estimated annual yield sits around 20.4%. Although the fund's history is short, we can see that XPAY has done a solid job at keeping payouts within a consistent range since its inception. Despite the markets volatility and uncertainty over the last year, management has done a great job at providing steady income for shareholders. Therefore, XPAY has proven to be a great option for investors seeking a reliable stream of income so far.

However, I worry that payouts may ultimately decline over time if the fund's assets don't substantially increase. As already established, NAV erosion may occur when the fund pays out more than it actually earns. Well, XPAY's NAV may decline with every payout, since return of capital distributions come directly from the NAV. As the NAV declines, the fund's size of positioning becomes smaller, which means that it will earn less from its flexible options.

On the plus side, the fund continues to prioritize tax-efficient distributions over time. For instance, the most recent section19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the last payout was classified as return of capital. Return of capital distributions are not classified as income and therefore, are not taxed as such. Instead, return of capital distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. This provides flexibility for XPAY to be held within a regular taxable brokerage account with little tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that XPAY only remains a hold at this time. The fund has proven to be more effective than 0DTE peers like XDTE and SDTY, but still not as effective over a longer holding period compared to funds like GPIX and SPYI. XPAY offers investors a choice somewhere in the middle of the road by providing an annualized yield of 20%, while partially participating in the upside growth of the market. Although the fund has some drawbacks, it can still be a long-term holding for income investors seeking tax-efficient income from the S&P 500.

data.title: Fidelity International Small Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the three months ending December 31, 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 1.76%, trailing the 2.98% advance of the benchmark MSCI All Country World Index ex U.S. Small Cap Net MA Linked Index.

Importantly, given our focus, long-term performance favors the fund over the benchmark.

After spending much of October and November below break-even, international small-cap stocks rallied to end the quarter with a modest gain. The gain was fueled by the generative AI boom, anticipation of fiscal stimulation in Europe, Japan and China, easing trade concerns and two interest-rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve.

During the period, investors flocked to low-quality, speculative stocks amid the U.S. Federal Reserve's return to cutting short-term interest rates. Lower interest rates are broadly stimulative for economic activity, but particularly helpful to firms with significant debt on their balance sheets, much of which tends to be in adjustable-rate loans.

Within the small-cap benchmark, the materials sector (+10%) posted the strongest return, followed by energy (+7%), information technology and financials (both +5%). Within technology, the strongest subsector, by far, was semiconductors & semiconductor equipment (+17%), which was aided by the generative AI spending surge. Conversely, communication services (-4%) had the weakest return, with health care and consumer staples (-2% each) lagging, as well. Consumer discretionary (-1%) also finished the quarter with a negative return. Looking at the major regions in the benchmark, Canada (+11%) had the best result. Asia Pacific ex Japan, Europe ex U.K. and the U.K. all posted a roughly 4% advance, while emerging markets rose 2% and Japan was up 1%.

Turning to the fund, stock selection and an underweight in Canada notably detracted versus the benchmark. Picks in the U.K., an underweight in Israel and positioning in Taiwan also hurt. Among sectors, lighter-than-index exposure to and investment choices in materials weighed on our relative result, along with picks in industrials and energy.

On a stock-specific basis, outsized exposure to Baycurrent (BYCCF) (-29%) detracted more than any other holding. Shares of the Japan-based provider of business strategy consulting, IT consulting, and IT integration declined sharply in October after the company reported disappointing results for its fiscal second quarter ending August 31. We considered this a temporary headwind and point out that Baycurrent's sales increased by a healthy 27% in the first half of the fiscal year, while net income rose by 29%.

Out-of-benchmark exposure to Rheinmetall (RNMBY) (-21%) also worked against the fund. The fundamental backdrop for the German maker of military vehicles and weapons continued to be constructive, in our view. With that said, after a huge rise in the first half of 2025, the stock was probably due for some consolidation.

On the positive side, investment choices in emerging markets, especially China and India, added value. By sector, picks in financials – notably the financial services industry – stood out to the upside.

Leading the way among individual holdings was a non-index stake in Shriram Finance (SHGAY) (+60%), which claims to be the second-largest retail non-banking financial company in India by loan balance, with a nationwide network of nearly 3,200 branches, mainly in rural and semi-urban regions. The company focuses on commercial- and passenger-vehicle loans, with a "strong presence" in used-vehicle loans. During the quarter, Japan's Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG)'s consolidated subsidiary MUFG Bank (MUFG) signed an agreement to acquire a 20% stake in the firm.

Also lifting the fund's relative result was RHI Magnesita (RHMGF) (+35%). The Austria-based seller of refractory products used in high-temperature industrial processes demonstrated resilience in Q3 2025 and reaffirmed its full-year guidance helping to lift its shares. The company highlighted cost efficiencies and small pricing increases, as well as the firm's efforts in integrating robotics solutions, which are gaining traction in the U.S., India, and Germany.

Outlook and Positioning

We expect the recent speculative phase being embraced by market participants to exhaust itself before long, at which point we believe investors will likely shift their attention from low-quality stocks to those of higher quality, as measured by fundamentals such as revenue and earnings growth, as well as the health of corporate balance sheets. This could benefit the fund over time, in our view.

It's worth mentioning that, as of year-end, valuations in the fund's international small-cap universe are much more reasonable than those of U.S. large-cap stocks. In fact, the latter are flirting with all-time highs seen at major market tops such as September 1929 and October 2007. In contrast, the fund's benchmark sported an average price-to-earnings valuation of only about 14x at the end of October, while the portfolio's average was even lower, at around 12x. Those numbers didn't change much by the end of 2025. Reasonable starting valuations provide more room for valuations to expand for companies supported by a solid fundamental backdrop.

Fidelity® International Small Cap Fund

Investment Approach

Fidelity® International Small Cap Fund is an opportunistic international small-cap strategy focused on our best ideas across geographies. The fund seeks capital appreciation.

We favor higher-quality small-cap companies trading at a discount to their intrinsic (fair) value because we believe this combination can lead to outperformance over the course of a market cycle.

This is a value-oriented approach where the fund looks to optimize the risk/reward trade-off by investing at the intersection of value and quality. Investing in "undiscovered" or "out of favor" securities is the key, in our view, to finding attractive valuations for "quality" businesses.

Typically, businesses we own require less capital to grow, which leads to strong free cash flow. The combination of strong free cash flow and an unencumbered balance sheet allows astute management teams the flexibility to deploy capital for inorganic (acquisition-driven) growth or returns to shareholders.

Definitions and Important Information

Unless otherwise expressly disclosed to you in writing, the information provided in this material is for educational purposes only. Any viewpoints expressed by Fidelity are not intended to be used as a primary basis for your investment decisions and are based on facts and circumstances at the point in time they are made and are not particular to you. Accordingly, nothing in this material constitutes impartial investment advice or advice in a fiduciary capacity, as defined or under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 or the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, both as amended. Fidelity and its representatives may have a conflict of interest in the products or services mentioned in this material because they have a financial interest in the products or services and may receive compensation, directly or indirectly, in connection with the management, distribution, and/or servicing of these products or services, including Fidelity funds, certain third-party funds and products, and certain investment services. Before making any investment decisions, you should take into account all of the particular facts and circumstances of your or your client's individual situation and reach out to a professional adviser, if applicable.

CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share Growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity. Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Important Fund Information

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (INDEX) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Fidelity International Small Cap Fund Linked Index represents the performance of the MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index) ex USA Small Cap Index since April 1, 2014, and the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index prior to that date.

MSCI All Country World ex USA Small Cap Net MA (1-Apr-2014)

Linked Index represents the performance of the MSCI All Country World ex US Small Cap (Net MA) since April 01, 2014, and MSCI All Country World Small Cap ex US (GroSs) prior to that date.

Market-segment Weights

Market-segment weights illustrate examples of sectors or industries in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any sector or industry.

Ranking Information

© 2026 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. The Morningstar information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or redistributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Fidelity does not review the Morningstar data and, for mutual fund performance, you should check the fund's current prospectus for the most up-to-date information concerning applicable loads, fees and expenses.

% Rank in Morningstar Category is the fund's total-return percentile rank relative to all funds that have the same Morningstar Category. The highest (or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest (or least favorable) percentile rank is 100. The top-performing fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1%. % Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges. Multiple share classes of a fund have a common portfolio but impose different expense structures.

Relative Weights

Relative weights represents the % of fund assets in a particular market segment, asset class or credit quality relative to the benchmark. A positive number represents an overweight, and a negative number is an underweight. The fund's benchmark is listed immediately under the fund name in the Performance Summary.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN Statistics

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Views expressed are through the end of the period stated and do not necessarily represent the views of Fidelity. Views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and Fidelity disclaims any responsibility to update such views. These views may not be relied on as investment advice and, because investment decisions for a Fidelity fund are based on numerous factors, may not be relied on as an indication of trading intent on behalf of any Fidelity fund. The securities mentioned are not necessarily holdings invested in by the portfolio manager(s) or FMR LLC. References to specific company securities should not be construed as recommendations or investment advice.

Other third-party marks appearing herein are the property of their respective owners.

data.title: Flux Power: Sell On Dismal Sales Outlook And Impending Debt Covenant Breach
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Flux Power Holdings Inc. or "Flux Power" (FLUX) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Three months ago, I upgraded shares of lithium-ion battery manufacturer Flux Power from "Sell" to "Buy" after an equity offering seemingly addressed the company's long-standing debt and liquidity issues and removed the threat of a Nasdaq delisting.

Unfortunately, I underestimated the company's persistent operational challenges as disclosed in Flux Power's regulatory filings:

In the second half of fiscal 2025, we experienced a slow down in new orders for our energy storage solutions, reflecting corresponding deferrals of new forklift purchases by selected large customer fleets due to lower capital spending and interest rate variability, and more recently, global tariff uncertainties. (...)

In addition, customers are placing fewer new orders for fiscal 2026. Some customers have attributed lower capital spending to concerns over the economy and the uncertainty of higher interest rates, as well as broader geopolitical uncertainty.

More recently, the economic impacts and costs of higher global tariffs implemented by the U.S. government have negatively impacted new purchase orders.

Following a disappointing Q1/FY2026 report in November, the stock price has declined by more than 50%:

After the close of Thursday's session, Flux Power reported mixed second-quarter results, with revenues coming in below consensus expectations. Despite less-than-stellar sales, the company managed to generate positive net income for the first time.

On the conference call, management attributed higher-than-expected profitability to several factors:

  • improved product mix
  • recent cost-savings initiatives
  • lower warranty costs
  • a $0.5 million one-time benefit related to the reversal of previously accrued employee bonus awards

So much for the good news. Unfavorable working capital movements resulted in negative free cash flow of $5.3 million, and the backlog of just $3.8 million declined to a new multi-year low.

Adding insult to injury, management warned of "materially lower revenue" in the current quarter after the company's largest customer implemented a "capital freeze":

However, recently, our most significant customer has conveyed to us that they are implementing a capital freeze. We are not certain how long this freeze will be in effect, but anticipate it may impact a significant portion of calendar year 2026. (...)

As a result, we expect materially lower revenue in our third quarter.

As disclosed in the company's quarterly report on Form 10-Q, two customers accounted for $11.0 million, or 78%, of revenues in the second quarter. With the largest customer no longer placing orders, I would expect a $6+ million negative impact on quarterly sales going forward.

As a result, FY2026 revenues will be nowhere near the numbers projected by analysts:

At quarterly revenue levels around or even below $10 million, Flux Power would suffer steep losses and meaningful cash outflows again.

Not surprisingly, management warned of impending debt covenant breaches and resulting substantial doubt regarding the company's ability to continue as a going concern:

While we are in compliance with debt covenants under the GBC Credit Facility as of December 31, 2025 and up through and including the date of filing of this Report on Form 10-Q, we expect to be subject to a compliance covenant breach under the GBC Credit Facility in late February 2026.

Therefore, we will need to negotiate an amendment to the GBC Credit Facility in order to have the ability to draw funds under the facility. Because successful negotiation of an amendment cannot be guaranteed and we may lose access to the line of credit under the GBC Credit Facility after the covenant breach, substantial doubt exists about our ability to continue as a going concern over the 12 months following the filing date of this Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q.

While proceeds from recent equity and warrant offerings have been used to lower outstanding debt quite meaningfully in recent months, the dismal sales outlook will result in increased funding needs.

Even if Flux Power manages to amend the terms of its up to $16 million credit facility with Gibraltar Business Capital ("GBC"), covenant relief would likely come at a price, particularly when considering the company's weak outlook.

While management expects the customer to resume orders at some point going forward, this is not a given by any means, as Flux Power is facing heavy competition from large companies like EnerSys (ENS) and Exide Technologies, as well as its closest peer, Electrovaya (ELVA).

At least in my opinion, given Flux Power's persistent challenges, management should consider putting the company on the auction block. However, finding a suitor might be difficult in the current market environment.

While Flux Power is changing hands at below 1x EV/Sales now, the company's prospects have worsened considerably in recent months.

For my part, I would not be surprised to see shares dropping below Nasdaq's $1 minimum bid price requirement over the next couple of weeks, which would revive the Nasdaq delisting threat.

In addition, the inability to come to terms with lender GBC could result in the requirement to dilute shareholders even further. Under a worst case scenario, Flux Power might be required to file for bankruptcy.

Given these issues, I am downgrading the company's common shares from "Buy" to "Sell" and apologize for a terrible call.

Risks

Considering Flux Power's dismal outlook, it is difficult to come up with credible scenarios for a sustained near-term move in the stock. However, new order announcements or news of the company pursuing strategic alternatives could result in shares catching another bid.

Bottom Line

Flux Power reported mixed second-quarter results with disappointing sales offset by much better-than-expected profitability as the company benefited from recent cost reduction initiatives and recognized a sizeable one-time gain from the reversal of previously accrued bonus awards.

On the flip side, unfavorable working capital movements resulted in substantial cash outflows, and the backlog declined to new multi-year lows.

Even worse, the company's largest customer won't place new orders for the time being due to the implementation of a "capital freeze". As a result, sales will be impacted materially for at least the next couple of quarters.

Adding insult to injury, Flux Power expects to violate debt covenants later this month. The issue has raised substantial doubt about the company's ability to continue as a going concern.

Given the company's mounting challenges, I am downgrading Flux Power's common shares from "Buy" to "Sell".

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CIO Notebook: January Jobs Prove Worth The Wait
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA

In terms of positioning, we remain constructive on risk assets, encouraging further diversification in both sector and geography to ensure exposure to cyclical areas of the global economy.

January non-farm payrolls were released today, five days later than anticipated due to a short partial shutdown of the U.S. federal government. While non-farm payrolls were expected to increase by +65k, whispers were for a potentially much lower print, particularly given the weak alternative labor data points released last week.

Instead, January payrolls were up a solid +130k, with November and December reflecting only modest downward revisions of -15k and -2k, respectively. However, aggregate non-farm employment for 2025 was revised down meaningfully – from +584k to +181k – implying a monthly average payroll gain of only +15k for the year, making it the third weakest year for employment since 2003.

Breaking down the data, payroll prints were once again led by health care (+82k) and social assistance (+42k). More interesting were the gains in construction (+33k) and manufacturing (+5k), particularly given the widespread weather disruptions in the month.

The gains in these sectors appear to corroborate the move higher in ISM Manufacturing and could reflect the underlying economic momentum that we believe will strengthen from current levels. Offsetting these areas was a decline in financial activities hiring (-22k) and continued payroll losses within the federal government (-34k).

A decline in the unemployment rate to 4.3% from 4.4%, even with a pickup in the participation rate from 62.4% to 62.5%, was particularly notable; employed persons rose by +528k while unemployed persons fell by -141k. Of note, the unemployment rate improved across almost all demographic categories in the month as well, an indication that broader improvement may be at hand. Average hourly earnings rose by +0.4% month-over-month and +3.7% year-over-year, while average hours worked were up as well to 34.3 from 34.2.

Expectations for the next Fed cut are reflecting the relative improvement indicated by today’s report, with higher probability afforded to a July interest rate cut. We would caution to jump to such a conclusion, however, ahead of Friday’s January CPI report.

While admittedly this print was better-than-anticipated, the labor market remains in tension, and the Fed has acknowledged that there is room to move rates lower absent an unexpected spike in inflation.

Our takeaway is that today’s report reflects several knowns – the U.S. labor market was softening throughout 2025, payrolls were overstated, and payrolls that were added were heavily concentrated in non-discretionary services. There is also evidence of (at the very least) delayed hiring in anticipation of productivity enhancing AI implementation.

With our expectation for strong U.S. growth (+2.5%, with a risk to the upside), we believe the labor market will continue to improve throughout 2026 as layoffs remain muted and hiring ticks up on the back of rebounding activity. This should translate into stronger consumption, buoyed by increased tax refunds, as well as productive capital expenditure outside of AI suppliers.

As such, we remain constructive on risk assets, encouraging further diversification in both sector and geography to ensure exposure to cyclical areas of the global economy.

Important Information

This material is provided for informational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice, or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold a security. This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types. Diversification does not guarantee profit or protect against loss in declining markets. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Investments in private equity are speculative and involve a higher degree of risk than more traditional investments. Investments in private equity are intended for sophisticated investors only. Unless otherwise indicated, returns shown reflect reinvestment of dividends and distributions. Indexes are unmanaged and are not available for direct investment. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Portfolio positioning views expressed herein are those of Neuberger Berman’s Private Wealth Investment Group, which may include those of the Neuberger Berman’s Asset Allocation Committee. Asset allocation and positioning views are based on a hypothetical reference portfolio. The Private Wealth Investment Group analyzes market and economic indicators to develop asset allocation strategies. The Private Wealth Investment Group works in partnership with the Office of the CIO. The Private Wealth Investment Group also consults regularly with portfolio managers and investment officers across the firm. The Asset Allocation Committee is comprised of professionals across multiple disciplines, including equity and fixed income strategists and portfolio managers. The Asset Allocation Committee reviews and sets long-term asset allocation models, establishes preferred near-term tactical asset class allocations and, upon request, reviews asset allocations for large, diversified mandates. Asset Allocation Committee members are polled on asset classes and the positional views are representative of an Asset Allocation Committee consensus. The views of the Asset Allocation Committee and the Private Wealth Investment Group may not reflect the views of the firm as a whole and Neuberger Berman advisers and portfolio managers may take contrary positions to the views of the Asset Allocation Committee or the Private Wealth Investment Group. The Asset Allocation Committee and the Private Wealth Investment Group views do not constitute a prediction or projection of future events or future market behavior. Positioning views may change over time without notice and actual client positioning may vary significantly. Such asset classes, such as equities and fixed income, may have significantly different overall risk-return characteristics which should be consider before investing.

data.title: Saratoga Investment: SAV, New BDC Bond With A 7.5% Yield
data.description:
Thesis

It has been a busy few months for many BDCs and CEFs, with many bond refinancings taking place. The companies are taking advantage of lower front rates and low credit spreads in order to lock in new, cheaper financing. This has translated into lower duration for investors, but also the loss of attractive rates and yields on many bonds.

Today we are going to have a look at Saratoga Investment Corp. (SAR), a business development company that just issued a new bond.

Who is Saratoga?

As per the Seeking Alpha landing page, Saratoga:

Is a business development company specializing in leveraged and management buyouts, acquisition financings, growth financings, recapitalization, debt refinancing, and transitional financing transactions at the lower end of middle market companies. The fund structures its investments as debt and equity by investing through first and second lien loans, mezzanine debt, co-investments, select high yield bonds, senior secured bonds, unsecured bonds, and preferred and common equity.

The BDC is smaller in size than its peers, with a market capitalization for its equity of roughly $377 M. Its performance in the past five years has been good:

The BDC managed to maintain a fairly stable price for its common equity, all while disbursing a high dividend yield. The name has done well on a relative value basis as well, outperforming an index of peers:

The company shows its performance versus the S&P BDC Index, which can be found here.

The BDC is fairly diversified, as opposed to some of its peers, which are sometimes focused only on a couple of market segments:

The company's earnings presentation shows their investments across 41 distinct industries.

The company has a balance sheet of roughly $1 billion, with over 99% of the underlying loans in the performing category:

Please note that while the investment portfolio is roughly $1 billion, the net assets are much lower given the leverage the BDC runs.

Balance sheet composition and leverage

The BDC's leverage is composed of the following public and private facilities:

The BDC runs bank credit facilities, SBA Debentures, baby bonds, and private notes.

The BDC is constrained by regulatory leverage ratios but does run a gearing factor that is on the high side as per its latest financials:

As a BDC, we are only allowed to employ leverage to the extent that our asset coverage, as defined in the 1940 Act, equals at least 200% after giving effect to such leverage, or, 150% if certain requirements under the 1940 Act are met. On April 16, 2018, as permitted by the Small Business Credit Availability Act, which was signed into law on March 23, 2018, our board of directors, including a majority of our directors who are not “interested persons” (as defined in Section 2(a)(19) of the 1940 Act”) of the Company (“independent directors”), approved a minimum asset coverage ratio of 150%, which became effective on April 16, 2019. The amount of leverage that we employ at any time depends on our assessment of the market and other factors at the time of any proposed borrowing. Our asset coverage ratio, as defined in the 1940 Act, was 168.4% as of November 30, 2025 and 162.9% as of February 28, 2025.

A lower coverage ratio means gearing is high. Many of the BDC's peers run coverage ratios above 190%, although others do not rely on SBC funding. If the BDC were to breach its coverage ratio, the regulatory framework kicks in to ensure debt holders are protected. Under Section 18 of the 1940 Act, a BDC cannot declare or pay a dividend on its common stock if its asset coverage falls below the required threshold. This would translate into the common equity having a massive leg down, while the debt would be helped by the additional cash coverage. Furthermore, the BDC could not borrow more, effectively being forced to sell assets (i.e., deleverage).

Baby bonds have passed their call dates

To note that most of the Saratoga baby bonds outstanding have passed their call date thus can now be redeemed at any time:

While the coupons are high, investors need to run yield-to-call calculations since many of them are trading above par, hence resulting in punitive yield-to-call figures.

The characteristics of the new bond are as follows:

  • Notional: $100 M
  • Coupon: 7.5%
  • Maturity date: Feb 6, 2031
  • Call date: Feb 6, 2028

The bonds just broke for trading and are priced at par; thus, the yield calculations are very simple since they equal the 7.5% coupon.

What an investor gets here is a secure yield for two years, with the first call date not until February 2028. The market is pricing for lower Fed Funds, which means down the line in 2026 the company might be able to refinance existing debt for even lower rates; thus, we consider SAJ, SAY, and SAZ to be very much projected to be called in 2026.

Risk factors

The largest risk factor for buying SAV is a potential recession. Private credit (like BDCs) has been accused of closely managing their marks for their very illiquid investments, with a wide dispersion of prices. A true recession would bring about actual defaults and lower prices, thus stressing net asset values and coverage ratios. Illiquid investments also become an issue during a recession because there is little interest and low prices in such assets.

Conclusion

SAV is a new baby bond from Saratoga Investment Corporation. The bond has a 7.5% coupon, a 2031 maturity date, and a call date in February 2028. The name just broke for trading and benefits from the BDC regulatory protective framework. While the other Saratoga baby bonds are currently callable, SAV offers investors a defined yield for at least two years. We are buyers for SAV here.

data.title: Crocs: Uninspiring Sales, But The Stock Is Cheap (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
While tech stocks are spending 2026 in the penalty box due to overriding fears that AI is coming to eat software companies' lunch, beaten-down sectors like retail have gotten a bit of a facelift this year, as investors considered the fact that macro headwinds don't appear as sharp as initially feared.

Crocs (CROX), the popular maker of casual clogs, has quickly turned into a rebound play. Despite a string of recent weak quarters, the company's Q4 print came in much better than feared, and investors rallied around the company's guidance for core Crocs-branded sales to be approximately flat in FY26. Shares rose ~20% post-earnings, bringing Crocs' gains to over 10% over the past year. The question for investors now is, can the optimism in this name continue, or is this merely a dead-cat bounce that reflects investors' skittishness to invest in growth and tech stocks and reallocate to traditional sectors instead?

I last wrote a sell opinion on Crocs in October. At the time, Crocs was facing sharp revenue declines in both of its brands, Crocs and HEYDUDE. Since then, the company notes that holiday sales have come in much better than expected, rewarding the company especially for its focus on growing its footprint in international markets. It's expecting 2026 to be a year of stabilization for the Crocs brand, while it's still casting a dour forecast for its underperforming HEYDUDE subsidiary, which it purchased in 2021 and is quickly turning out to be a bad bet. Still, problems remain here, including waning margins, but given Crocs' very cheap relative valuation against the broader market, I'm raising my outlook on the stock to neutral.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for Crocs. On the bright side for this company:

  • Crocs is broadening its product footprint. Today, roughly three-quarters of Crocs' business is in its standard clogs. It's expanding its product line into sandals and close-toed shoes, which will help Crocs capture more of the global $380 billion footwear market.
  • International growth opportunities. The company is laser-focused on expanding its presence in China, Japan, and France: attractive and large international markets in which Crocs currently has less than <1% market share.

At the same time, however, I remain very concerned about several risks:

  • Crocs is facing severe gross margin slippage. Many consumer apparel stocks have held up surprisingly well in the face of tariffs. Crocs, however, is adding tariff burdens on top of already-weakening product margins, driven by poorer sales and the need to discount old stock.
  • HEYDUDE sales weakness. Even after a sharp ~18% sales decline in FY25, Crocs is still expecting the HEYDUDE brand to decline a further ~9% in FY26. This is incredibly poor performance for a company that Crocs spent $2.5 billion to acquire.
  • Crippling debt load. The company has over $1.2 billion in debt (~$1.1 billion in net debt, after taking out cash), and though the company has a weakening cash flow/profit margin position, it's buying back stock, which isn't helping the company to deleverage its balance sheet.

The main saving grace for Crocs is the fact that the stock sits at very cheap multiples. For FY26, Crocs is guiding to -1% to flat y/y revenue growth. The Crocs brand is expected to grow at 0-2% y/y, offset by -9% to -7% decline in HEYDUDE.

Pro forma EPS is expected at $12.88-$13.35 (we note that this includes $1.17 in pro forma add backs, with Crocs stripping out one-time expected charges for supply chain optimization investments), which positions Crocs' valuation at just 7.5x FY26 P/E.

Of course, that's an incredibly appealing multiple to invest in any stock. At the same time, Crocs is facing severe growth challenges and instability on margins that make it less of a surefire bet. In my view, I prefer to remain on the sidelines to invest elsewhere.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Crocs' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Crocs' revenue declined -3.3% y/y to $957.6 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $917.1 million (-7.3% y/y) by a massive four-point margin. Clearly, the Street (and Crocs itself) was expecting far worse holiday sales.

The chart below showcases Crocs' sales trends by brand, region, and channel. The Crocs brand declined -0.4% y/y in constant-currency terms. We note bright spots, however, in international sales, which grew 11% on an FX-neutral basis, buoyed by a 30% jump in direct-to-consumer sales.

Meanwhile, wholesalers in North America continue to pull back on orders, leading to a ~20% y/y revenue decline for the Crocs brand in the wholesale channel, while overall HEYDUDE revenue also fell -17.5% y/y in constant-currency terms.

The company believes that product newness helped to catalyze sales for Crocs during the holiday season. Per CEO Andrew Rees' remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Now turning to the Crocs Brand. We had a strong holiday season with positive consumer response to our new product introductions. International grew double digits and sales in North America outperformed our expectation [...]

In 2025, our style sandals led the way, fueled by strong full-price selling of our Brooklyn, Getaway and Miami franchises. While sandal awareness is roughly half that of clogs, we saw an encouraging mid-single-digit increase in sandal awareness during 2025 versus 2024. Looking forward into 2026, we believe continued newness in our existing franchises, along with the introduction of our new Saturday franchise and updated personalizable 2-strap sandal underscores an opportunity to gain further market share in this category."

The company notes as well that in January, it struck a fresh partnership with LEGO, creating a brand-new Lego Brick Clog" (retailing at $150), which may tap into LEGO's very popular brand to drive new customers for Crocs.

Still, we note that Crocs' margins are fading. Gross margins declined -330 bps y/y to 54.7%, which the company attributed to a -300 bps y/y tariff headwind. We note that this runs counter to many apparel companies, which have been able to offset tariffs by higher product margins (whether through higher pricing or manufacturing efficiencies).

The company also didn't manage to generate any savings from opex. Operating margins, meanwhile, also fell -340 bps y/y to 16.8%:

Crocs' free cash flow fell -19% y/y to $246.4 million:

Again, Crocs' debt load bears mentioning here. Its Q4 balance sheet reports $1.23 billion of debt (or ~$1.1 billion in net debt, after taking out $130 million in cash balances), which sits at nearly a 5x multiple of annual cash flow. It will take Crocs a substantial amount of time to bring its debt load down to reasonable levels (especially as we note that Crocs spent $582 million, or double its free cash flow, to buy back stock in FY25).

Key takeaways

There's an old saying here: "You get what you pay for." Crocs is optically cheap at an ~8x forward P/E multiple, but it's battling weak sales for both of its brands, waning gross margins, and declining free cash flow amid a substantial debt load. While Q4 sales provided an optimistic lift to sentiment for this stock, I think Crocs still has a long road to recovery ahead. Steer clear here for now and maintain caution from the sidelines.

data.title: Norwegian Cruise Line: Strong Wave Season, Discounted Shares Present Attractive Opportunity
data.description:
Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH) appears to be missing out on the investor enthusiasm relative to its peers. The stock is down over 12% over the past year, while peers such as Carnival (CCL) and Royal Caribbean (RCL) are up about 30% in the same period.

I believe the disconnect presents an attractive opportunity today for investors seeking a discounted name that I believe could see momentum higher on a strong overall wave season through 2026.

NCLH Stock Key Metrics

At current trading valuations, NCLH commands a forward multiple of just under 11x. This compares to a 13x and 18.5x multiple from CCL & RCL, respectively. Shares appear discounted through other measures as well. Another metric that catches my eye is its price-to-cash flow of just 5x. This is well below that of both CCL and RCL.

The discount follows years of underperformance relative to its peer set since the start of the COVID-19 pandemic. While CCL and RCL both benefitted from returning investors following the worst of the pandemic, NCLH was left in the basement dustbin. Shares are flat to negative over a five-year timespan, while CCL and RCL have turned in gains of 60% and 400%, respectively.

Is it time for a rebound? Analysts across the board broadly believe so. This includes the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants, which provide high marks in seemingly every measurable category.

Within the analyst community, Wall Street is highly bullish, with 50% of those covering the stock in the last 90 days rating shares as a ‘strong buy’. This is accompanied by an average price target of just shy of $30/share. I am within this camp, and I believe NCLH can reach its potential over the medium to long term.

NCLH Stock Operating Performance

Partly to blame for NCLH's underperformance are its weaker-than-expected results in Q3. NCLH’s latest results in Q3 disappointed investors due to a miss on both the top and bottom lines. The guidance was also not viewed favorably by the Wall Street crowd. I, however, would disagree with the negative sentiment.

For one, quarterly revenues reached an all-time high, driven by robust demand and higher load factors, which finished at a record 106.4%. That’s not too shabby for a stock down double digits over the past year, in my view.

The revenue strength flowed directly to the bottom line, as adjusted EBITDA surpassed +$1B for the first time in company history, while full-year adjusted EPS of $1.20/share came in $0.06/share ahead of expectations. This beat, however, was overshadowed by the miss for the quarter.

The management team also showed highly competent strategic execution, in my view. Net yields, for example, grew 1.5%, supported by pricing gains of more than 3%, while costs remained essentially flat YOY.

The combination of pricing and effective cost management drove adjusted operational EBITDA margins to 36.7% over the trailing twelve months, representing a 220 basis point improvement from last year and continued progress toward management’s longer-term margin targets.

In terms of booking momentum, I’m bullish as NCLH heads into its Q4 release at the end of the month. Q3 marked the strongest third-quarter booking performance in company history, with bookings up more than 20% YOY.

In addition, demand remained strong into the early part of Q4, driven by family-focused Caribbean itineraries and continued strength across its luxury brands. Management’s strategic pivot toward short sailings, improved guest experiences, and enhanced onboard monetization appears to be paying dividends, in my view.

What Is The Outlook For NCLH Stock?

As mentioned earlier, I’m bullish on the forward outlook for a number of reasons. First, forward guidance provided at the end of Q3 was positive, even if it disappointed Wall Street. There, the management team guided for operating momentum to carry through the remainder of 2025 and accelerate further into 2026, driven by rising load factors, disciplined pricing, and continued cost control.

For Q4, specifically, the company is guiding to occupancy approaching 102%, with net yields growing 3.5% to 4% and EBITDA of roughly +$555M. More importantly, management expects load factors to climb another 200 to 300 basis points in early 2026 as short Caribbean sailings expand and new amenities at Great Stirrup Cay come online.

Aside from the guidance, I believe industry conditions remain supportive as well. Early indications point to a strong wave season, with bookings and pricing both running ahead of last year. RCL recently described wave season as “fantastic,” noting that roughly two-thirds of its 2026 capacity is already sold at record rates. Though this is coming from a competitor, I have high confidence that it applies sector-wide.

All considered, I believe that demand remains robust, pricing power is intact, and operators are filling ships further in advance. Combined with NCLH’s improving cost structure and expanding margins, I believe the company is well positioned to deliver another year of strong earnings growth in 2026.

Is NCLH Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I view NCLH as an attractive ‘buy’ at current levels.

The stock’s meaningful underperformance relative to peers has created a compelling valuation disconnect, one that I believe is increasingly difficult to justify given the company’s improving fundamentals, strong booking momentum, and positive industry outlook.

Trading at under 11x forward earnings and just 5x cash flow, I believe NCLH offers a rare opportunity to gain exposure to a lucrative recovery story at a discounted price.

Wall Street’s consensus price target of just under $30 implies more than 20% upside from current levels, and I believe that target is well within reach over the medium to long term.

With operating momentum building, margins expanding, and leverage steadily declining, I believe that the fundamentals are improving each quarter. At the same time, the industry appears to be entering a strong multi-year demand cycle, supported by an encouraging wave season and rising forward bookings.

Near term, I am bullish heading into earnings, as I believe the market continues to underestimate both the strength of demand and the durability of pricing. Longer term, I expect shares to rebound and performance to narrow relative to CCL and RCL.

For investors seeking an attractively valued way to play the ongoing cruise recovery, NCLH stands out as a compelling opportunity, in my view.

data.title: Will Crypto ETFs Have Lasting Appeal?
data.description:
By Ivan Castano

Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) price may have fallen from last year's highs, but that's not stopping a slew of ETFs from courting investors in hopes that the king of crypto will rebound this year.

Regulatory tailwinds, such as the SEC's recent passage of generic listings standards (GLS) and the upcoming The Digital Asset Market Clarity (CLARITY) Act, are also expected to boost investor interest in these funds, which raised a whopping $47.2 billion last year, despite $5 billion of withdrawals in the fourth quarter.

Traditional finance (TradFi) remains bullish on digital currencies, and many institutions are set to enter the space in the coming months, potentially helping ETF inflows to more than double in 2026, analysts say.

"We will absolutely see more inflows this year," said Chris Matta, CEO of consultancy Cryptocollective.io, adding that he expects 25 to 50 new such funds to launch this year. "Though BTC went from roughly $120,000 to $90,000 last year, you still had a huge amount of inflows."

Institutions such as banks and asset managers have warmed up to digital currencies amid Washington's policy changes and GLS's rollout. The provisions have been a boon to the $3.3 trillion industry, sharply streamlining ETF listings and sparking a second wave of applications featuring popular altcoins such as Doge (DOGE-USD), Cardano (ADA-USD) and Polkadot (DOT-USD).

This momentum is also being reflected in the derivatives market. In February, CME Group expanded its crypto derivatives suite with the launch of Cardano, Chainlink and Stellar futures. This comes off the back of a year of records for the exchange's crypto product suite, with total notional volume up 75% in 2025 vs 2024.

Regulatory Shifts

Before GLS, crypto ETFs were treated as "special cases" requiring cumbersome filings subject to long delays. Under the new rules, if a crypto asset meets certain preset criteria, an exchange can list it within five days and without an SEC vote.

GLS requires a coin to be traded as a futures asset for at least six months, have a 12-month average liquidity of $700 million and be part of the fraud-preventing Intermarket Surveillance Group (ISG) to gain approval.

The regulation has encouraged a string of banks, such as Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs, to launch their own crypto ETFs. It has also given rise to so-called basket ETFs incorporating diverse coins, with the Nasdaq Crypto US Index (NCIUS) (which holds Bitcoin, Ethereum (ETH-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), XRP (XRP-USD), Cardano and more) being a key example. In January of this year, it was announced that the Nasdaq Crypto US Index would become the Nasdaq CME Crypto Index (NCI).

“The announcement brings together two of the world’s most trusted market infrastructure providers at a pivotal moment for the digital asset ecosystem, as renewed investor confidence and clearer regulatory frameworks accelerate institutional participation,” the Nasdaq Newsroom reported.

Institutional Adoption

Meanwhile, U.S. banks and asset managers have begun advising clients to hold crypto as part of a diversified portfolio.

The latest is Bank of America, which is allowing its network of 15,000 advisors to recommend spot Bitcoin ETFs. It is also advising clients to hold 1% to 4% of total assets in crypto, following on the heels of similar moves by Morgan Stanley, Fidelity, JP Morgan and Wells Fargo.

Morgan Stanley recently announced the launch of a Solana ETF with staking rewards. Called the Morgan Stanley Solana Trust, the vehicle pays 6.5% to 7.7% annually into the fund. ETF issuers are adding proof-of-stake (PoS) rewards that help run coin networks to boost their funds' appeal. These can be paid into the fund or through an annual investor dividend.

The SEC is currently reviewing applications for a handful of other vehicles, including those for Doge, Cardano, Polkadot and Avalanche (AVAX-USD). As one of the first major altcoins, Cardano is known for its ultra-secure network and reliable 'liquid staking.' Meanwhile, Polkadot and Avalanche distinguish themselves by offering faster transaction speeds, lower costs and higher staking rewards.

Clarity in Focus

Looking at future regulations, all eyes are currently set on the CLARITY Act, the administration's latest regulation that could benefit crypto. Currently in Congress, the bill is expected to win approval in the first half.

Within CLARITY, digital assets will be classified as "Digital Commodities" under the CFTC instead of as "securities" under the SEC's purview. This means fund issuers won't have to worry about being sued for holding 'unregistered securities.'

The provision also lowers the regulatory burden for banks to hold crypto ETFs as custodians, encouraging conservative pension funds and sovereign wealth funds to enter the space, experts say. CLARITY also calls for ETFs to be treated like stablecoins, enabling real time, 24/7 settlement.

"CLARITY is going to help bolster trading and custody for Bitcoin," said Bitcoin consultant Mark Connors. “As banks begin to act as custodians, they will get their trading, research and advisory arms behind the business, deepening adoption."

CLARITY is facing some speed bumps, however. The Senate rejected it on January 16 over a dispute concerning stablecoin yields, casting uncertainty on whether it will be legislated before the U.S.'s mid-term elections.

Crypto-focused lawyer Carol Van Cleef said the CLARITY Act's delay is unlikely to dent Bitcoin's long-term prospects. She added that other factors, such as volatility surrounding U.S. foreign policy, could play a bigger role.

"CLARITY will give Bitcoin a shot in the arm but there are so many factors in the environment that could affect its price," she said. "We have seen a lot of technological developments and use cases have exploded in the last few years, all without a major overhaul of the regulatory structures."

If CLARITY passes and other crypto catalysts, such as the U.S.'s growing twin deficits – simultaneous budget and trade deficits – and expectations of two more rate cuts this year, materialize, Bitcoin could reverse course.

Amid this potential momentum, Connors forecasts that ETF inflows could gain 1x to 1.5x this year. And in 2027, as more institutions and sovereign wealth funds move into the space, they could swell by 1.5x to 2x, he said.

Last year, sovereign funds, notably Qatar's QIA, Norway's NBIM and Abu Dhabi's ADIA/Mubdala, purchased BTC directly or via stakes in BTC proxies like MicroStrategy. Analysts expect the trend to continue over the next 12-24 months.

Bear Market?

But critics say not so fast. In a recent report, CryptoQuant said Bitcoin has entered a bear market, adding that spot Bitcoin funds cut their holdings by about 24,000 BTC in the fourth quarter of 2025 and that it doesn't expect those flows to recover fast.

Bitcoin fell below its 365-day moving average ($101,000) last November, a level the consultancy said has historically signaled a bear market. Even after bouncing roughly 21% by early January, it still remained technically under water, the consultancy added.

But Kat Liu, a portfolio manager at ETF manager Ipox Schuster, is unconcerned. "If it doesn't go lower than $60,000, I wouldn't worry," she said. "BTC is less speculative than five years ago. Institutional investors, pension funds and hedge funds still want to get in."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
